articles.topic: Video gaming boosts memory attention reasoning
articles.headline: Your Brain Might Actually Love Gaming More Than You Think
articles.body: A massive review of 133 studies found that video game players consistently outperform non-gamers across multiple cognitive abilities. Memory showed the strongest improvements in controlled trials, while spatial reasoning and mental flexibility also improved. The pattern held regardless of age, sex, or game type. Researchers think the neuroplasticity benefits come from practicing impulse control and sustained attention while gaming. It's not about flashy scores—it's serious cognitive training disguised as play.
articles.takeaway: Regular gaming improves memory, attention, and reasoning. If gaming feels productive for your brain, that's because it actually is.

articles.topic: Hidden bacteria CAG-170 linked to good health
articles.headline: Scientists Just Found a Mystery Bacteria That Thrives in Healthy People
articles.body: A Cambridge-led study of over 11,000 gut samples across 39 countries identified a previously unknown bacteria called CAG-170 that appears far more often in healthy individuals. People with chronic diseases had significantly lower levels. This bacteria produces vitamin B12 and helps support the broader gut ecosystem. The discovery could reshape how we understand what makes a gut truly healthy. Researchers are now working to develop new methods to grow and study these beneficial microbes.
articles.takeaway: A mystery gut bacteria shows up consistently in healthy people worldwide. This finding could lead to better probiotic strategies.

articles.topic: Brain training improves cognition at any age
articles.headline: You Can Get Sharper. A Three-Year Study Proves It Works at Any Age.
articles.body: A landmark study of 4,000 adults aged 19 to 94 shows that cognitive decline is not inevitable. Participants who spent 5 to 15 minutes daily on targeted brain training improved measurably over three years. The research used the BrainHealth Index, a first-of-its-kind metric measuring executive function, learning, and emotional regulation. Even during major life stressors, participants demonstrated recovery through cognitive strategies. The takeaway: your brain remains trainable and rewireable at every stage of life.
articles.takeaway: Regular brain training works. Small daily habits can improve cognition measurably, even for people in their 90s.

articles.topic: Hormone therapy protects postmenopausal bone health
articles.headline: Hormone Therapy Cuts Bone Loss Risk By Two-Thirds. Here's What That Means.
articles.body: New research shows women taking menopausal hormone therapy had a 69% lower risk of low bone mineral density compared to those without treatment. This matters because bone loss accelerates sharply after menopause. The study examined data from thousands of women and found hormone therapy, especially low-dose transdermal or vaginal formulations, can meaningfully preserve bone strength. While bone health wasn't previously a top selling point of HRT, it's emerging as a significant benefit for women choosing treatment during and after menopause.
articles.takeaway: Menopausal hormone therapy significantly protects bone density, a major predictor of long-term independence and quality of life.

product.name: Thorne Vitamin D3/K2
product.blurb: Most adults are deficient. D3 with K2 is the combination that actually works.

quote.text: Spontaneity is great but life is just easier and everything goes far better when we have protocols.
quote.author: Andrew Huberman
quote.context: Neuroscientist and Stanford professor specializing in brain plasticity and performance optimization.

workout.title: Progressive Stride Ladder: Build Speed with Recovery Anchors
workout.tip: Treat each fast segment as a separate effort—don't carry fatigue from one repeat into the next. If you feel sluggish, extend the recovery jog by 15 seconds. It's better to nail fewer repeats cleanly than to grind through tired ones.
workout.warmup: 4 min: easy jog, then 5 x 20m striders with 40m walk recovery (focus on form, not speed)

exercises.name: Stride Ladder: 3-min, 2-min, 1-min Repeats
exercises.reps: 3 min hard / 2 min easy jog / 2 min hard / 2 min easy jog / 1 min hard / 2 min easy jog
exercises.notes: Work at 80–85% max effort on each hard segment (a pace you could hold for maybe 5–6 minutes). Easy jogs should genuinely feel easy—your heart rate should drop noticeably. The 'ladder down' format lets your nervous system recover between efforts while building stamina.

workout.cooldown: 3 min easy jog, then 2 min walk. Standing hamstring fold 25 sec, quad stretch 20 sec each leg, hip flexor lunge stretch 20 sec each side.
workout.description: An interval session that mixes short, fast strides with consistent easy-jog recovery periods to introduce speed training without fatigue overload. This format teaches pacing rhythm and is ideal for beginners prepping for 5K and 10K races.

meal.title: Whole Grain Toast with Whipped Ricotta, Figs & Pistachios
meal.description: A Mediterranean-inspired open-faced toast that pairs slow-digesting whole grains with creamy ricotta and natural carbs from figs—perfect pre-endurance fuel that won't spike and crash your blood sugar. The healthy fats from pistachios help with satiety and nutrient absorption during long efforts.
ingredients: 2 slices whole grain or sourdough bread
meal.instructions:
1. Toast bread to your preferred crispness—about 2–3 minutes.
2. While bread toasts, stir ricotta with lemon zest and a pinch of salt until smooth and airy.
3. Spread whipped ricotta generously on each toast.
4. Top with fresh figs (halved) or chopped dried figs.
5. Drizzle with honey and scatter pistachios over top.
6. Serve immediately.
meal.tip: Make whipped ricotta the night before and store in an airtight container—spreads even easier and saves 3 minutes on weekday mornings. Dried figs work year-round and are more stable for meal prep.
